title: Will India Shed Tears, at Least for Isaipriya's Death?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Isaipriya!

Even the name is beautiful! And her face was the face of a child. One could have taken her for a girl returning from college. She was only twenty-seven. The sin she committed by being born into this world was that she joined the media division of the Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam.

Rajapaksa must accept responsibility under international law and answer the nations of the world for cruelties without precedent in history: the shooting of Balachandran, the twelve-year-old son of LTTE leader Prabhakaran, and the slaughter of many thousands of children, women and elderly people.

That tender child Balachandran, born as Prabhakaran's son, was too young even to understand that he was about to be killed. Yet they fired five bullets into his chest. We saw that horror, enough to make one's whole being tremble, and we cried out in anguish.

What were those terrible scenes filmed and produced, amid so many dangers, by Channel 4 Television, based in London? One showed Balachandran with a piece of bread between his hand and mouth, and the next showed him lying dead after taking bullets to his chest. A mother and child lay fallen, clasping each other. Innocent women had been stripped naked and shot dead. Men were stripped, blindfolded, their hands tied behind their backs, and shot in the back. Colonel Ramesh, one of the LTTE's commanders, lay dead with his face disfigured. We saw those horrors.

Yesterday, we were subjected to the agony of seeing the killing of the woman Tiger Isaipriya. At an age when other young women were immersed in countless dreams, that songbird carried in her heart the radiant dream of Tamil Eelam. The Sinhala army violated and murdered her.

Britain's Channel 4 television network has repeatedly released such heart-rending video footage of the Sri Lankan military's alleged war crimes, placing the cruelty of Sinhala thugs before the eyes of the world.
The Rajapaksa government had until now claimed that Isaipriya was killed in battle. The newly released footage of her killing exposes that story and deception with evidence.
Channel 4, which had already released footage of many Tamil women being sexually assaulted and killed by Sinhala soldiers, has now filmed and released the material concerning Isaipriya.

In the footage, Isaipriya attempts to escape across a broad expanse resembling a field. Sri Lankan soldiers seize and torture her. They make her sit on the wet ground. Soldiers approaching her give her a white cloth, cover her with it, and lead her away. One soldier points to Isaipriya and says she is Prabhakaran's daughter. Her voice can be heard denying it.
Footage of Isaipriya singing is also shown. A close friend had earlier said that Isaipriya, always moving briskly about with her pen and camera, would not even carry a gun for self-defence.

When a resolution against Sri Lanka came up for debate at the UN Human Rights Council, Channel 4 broadcast footage concerning young Balachandran, Prabhakaran's son. Now, as the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting is about to take place in Sri Lanka itself, the same Channel 4 has brought the Isaipriya footage before the whole world and left us sobbing in grief. After seeing this horrifying footage, do our Prime Minister, our External Affairs Minister, and those who still argue that India must attend that Commonwealth meeting continue to believe India should participate?
Are the atrocities committed against Eelam Tamils by Sinhala chauvinism only one or two in number?

Dhanapathi of Tiruvottiyur was a labourer. While he and his companions were returning from Kataragama, their bus broke down. Leaving it to be repaired, Dhanapathi and two others went to a barber shop. A mob of Sinhala fanatics approached carrying axes. The moment they saw him, they shouted, “Ah! There is a Tamil!” and sprang upon him like wolves upon a fawn. Blood gushed. They celebrated by bathing their axes in Tamil blood.
Here is another scene. A Tamil was killed, his body hacked into pieces, his flesh sliced into slabs and placed in a basket, beside a sign reading, “Tamil flesh for sale here.”

Can anyone forget Sinhala fanatics entering Colombo prison with deadly weapons and massacring thirty-seven Tamil Eelam liberation fighters? Thangathurai, a commanding leader of the struggle, the strategists Yogachandran, known as Kuttimani, and Jegan, and thirty-four other Tamil Eelam fighters were killed inside the prison in a sudden attack by Sinhala jackals.

When the judge sentenced Kuttimani and Thangathurai to death, Thangathurai said in court, “If we die, your laws will not be able to control the Tamil Eelam that will arise in the future.” In the same court, Kuttimani said, “I am no coward who fears death. After you hang me, remove my eyes and give them to a blind young man in Eelam. Even if I cannot see the free Tamil Eelam that will bloom in the future, I will rejoice to see it through the eyes placed in that young man.”

They were all murdered in their prison cells. Their killers asked, “Was it with these eyes that you wished to see Tamil Eelam?”, gouged those eyes out, threw them down and stamped upon them. That horror took place in this very Sri Lanka. And now they say the Commonwealth meeting will be held in that country. Some Tamils here still say India should participate. Does their blood not boil?

Have we forgotten the story of Baskaran? Who was Baskaran? He was a Tamil. Was that not enough? They tied him to a tree and sexually assaulted his wife, naked, in the middle of the street. Did those sinners stop there? They hung the naked woman upside down between two areca palms, tying one leg to one tree and the other to the next. They drew the treetops together and tied them, then suddenly cut the binding. As the two trees sprang apart, her body was torn in two and fell to the ground. All this took place before the eyes of her husband Baskaran. What crime had Baskaran committed? Only that he was born a Tamil.
The Commonwealth meeting is to be held in Colombo, where they lived. If India participates, will history not curse us by asking whether we are Tamils at all?

Sinhala majoritarianism treats the Tamil people, Sri Lanka's original inhabitants and descendants of those who once ruled the island, as insects, lowly creatures crawling in the dust and abandoned orphans. Seeing them tortured, crushed and destroyed, we in mother Tamil Nadu shed tears of blood. We demand justice from the international community that has witnessed the genocide of Tamils.

We continue to insist upon an independent and credible international investigation into the ethnic cleansing, human-rights violations and unprecedented war crimes committed in Eelam; punishment of those responsible; and a referendum under UN supervision so that Eelam Tamils may themselves choose the just political settlement they desire.

We will tirelessly continue the effort to rally international opinion behind these demands. After seeing the evidence of Isaipriya's destruction, Tamils here and throughout the world expect India to refuse to attend the Commonwealth meeting in Sri Lanka, both to condemn the Sinhala government responsible and to draw the world's attention to this heart-rending event.
What will the Indian government do? Even after the atrocity inflicted upon Isaipriya, must India go to Sri Lanka? Will India shed tears, at least for Isaipriya's death?
With affection,
M. K.
4 November 2013
